description: This figure presents a comprehensive EBSD and nanoindentation characterization matrix for low-density δ-ferrite steel processed at four rolling temperatures (R650, R750, R850, R950). The leftmost column (a–d) shows GB + GOS maps, where green regions denote GOS ≤ 2° and black/grey lines mark high-angle (>15°) and low-angle (2–15°) grain boundaries. The KAM maps (e–h) visualize kernel average misorientation (scale 0–4.99°), indicating stored dislocation density. The IPF maps (i–l) reveal grain orientation distributions relative to the rolling direction. The rightmost column (m–p) overlays nanoindentation hardness (NI) onto the microstructure. Visual trends and caption data together show that increasing rolling temperature raises the HAGB fraction from 43.5–49.6 % (low-temperature zone) to 60.1–75.7 % (high-temperature zone) and increases the GOS ≤ 2° proportion from 6.9–12.3 % to 19.4–25.8 %, while KAM values decrease from 0.48–0.55° to 0.3–0.4°, indicating progressive recrystallization and dislocation annihilation. Correspondingly, IPF textures shift from dominant <001> toward <101>. Nanoindentation maps report a small hardness mismatch (~0.72 GPa) between carbide/ferrite composite lamellae and δ-ferrite lamellae at low temperatures, whereas high-temperature processing produces a severe gradient (martensite 5.98–6.50 GPa vs. δ-ferrite 2.64–2.77 GPa, maximum difference 3.86 GPa). These results support the conclusion that low-temperature rolling preserves ultra-fine carbides that pin dislocations via the Orowan mechanism and promote deformation coordination, while high-temperature rolling induces extreme hardness mismatch at phase boundaries, creating preferential sites for crack initiation and impairing toughness.
